title: Publications
text:
My publication list is pulled automatically from my Google Scholar profile using R. If you are interested in an article that is not Open Access, please reach out.
The data and code used for my publications are archived on my Open Science Framework page.
PAGE UNDER CONSTRUCTION
Researcher h-index: 4
Total number of citations: 42


1. MN Dumas, B Krasnay, CA Chabot, M Fraser Franco, É Gagnon, ... (2026). Longevity is heritable and negatively genetically correlated between the sexes in yellow-bellied marmots. *Evolution Letters, qraf*.

2. MN Dumas, P Bize, JGA Martin (2025). Temporal changes in sex-specific cryptic sexual dimorphism and allometric scaling in the long-lived Alpine swift Tachymarptis melba. *Journal of Evolutionary Biology*.

3. MN Dumas, CM Meier, P Bize, JGA Martin (2025). Who keeps the house after divorcing? Partner and Nest fidelity in the long-lived Alpine swift. *The American Naturalist*.

4. MN Dumas, S St. Lawrence, G Masoero, P Bize, JGA Martin (2024). Adult body mass is heritable, positively genetically correlated and under selection of differing shapes between the sexes in a bird with little apparent sexual dimorphism. *Journal of Animal Ecology*.

5. G Masoero, MN Dumas, JGA Martin, P Bize (2024). Trait‐specific sensitive developmental windows: Wing growth best integrates weather conditions encountered throughout the development of nestling Alpine swifts. *Ecology and Evolution*.

6. MN Dumas, J Martin, P Bize (2024). Shared genetic architecture and sex-specific fitness consequences of forked tails in the Alpine swift Tachymarptis melba. *OSF*.

7. MN Dumas (2024). Sex-specific genetic architecture and fitness consequences of subtly dimorphic traits in the long-lived Alpine swift. *Université d'Ottawa| University of Ottawa*.

8. S St. Lawrence, MN Dumas, M Petelle, DT Blumstein, JGA Martin (2022). Sex-specific reproductive strategies in wild yellow-bellied marmots (Marmota flaviventer): senescence and genetic variance in annual reproductive success differ …. *Behavioral Ecology and Sociobiology*.

9. MC Lorenzi, D Schleicherová, FG Robles-Guerrero, M Dumas, A Araguas (2021). Egg-trading worms start reciprocation with caution, respond with confidence and care about partners’ quality. *Scientific Reports*.

title: Michela Dumas
text:
Hi there! I’m Michela.



Photo of Michela petting a cow in the Swiss Alps


I’m a quantitative ecologist with a particular interest for sexual selection. I aim to answer questions related to the evolution of sexual dimorphism in the wild using long-term individual-based datasets. I’m also broadly interested in the effects of climate change and direct human impacts on wild populations.
Currently, I am Wildlife Research Analyst with the Ontario Public Service (Ministry of Natural Resources). My work contributes to monitoring caribou populations in Ontario for the purpose of management and policy decision-making
Previously, I worked as a Postdoctoral Research Fellow at the University of Ottawa, where I built on work I undertook during my PhD under the supervision of Prof. Julien Martin (M.A.D. Lab, University of Ottawa, Canada) and Dr. Pierre Bize (Bize Lab, Swiss Ornithological Institute - Vogelwarte Sempach, Switzerland).
For more information on my work, check out my projects.
I spend most of my free time backpacking or otherwise enjoying the great outdoors. You’ll find a smattering of photos from some of my favourite adventures throughout this website!

Education
 Ph.D. in Biology University of Ottawa, Ontario, Canada. 2020-2024.
 M.Sc. in Ethology Université Paris Sorbonne Nord, Ile-de-France, France. 2018-2020.
 B.Sc. in Biology Honours, with speciallization in Ecology, Ecolution, and Behaviour, with a minor in Psychology. 2012-2017.
